FULL CONTENT:
The Oxford Handbook of Authoritarian Politics (In Progress) Anne Wolf (ed.) https://doi.org/10.1093/oxfordhb/9780198871996.001.0001 Published: 23 January 2024 Online ISBN: 9780191983078 Print ISBN: 9780198871996 Search in this book CHAPTER https://doi.org/10.1093/oxfordhb/9780198871996.013.1 Published: 20 February 2025 Abstract Keywords: modes of conceptualization, regimes, scales, typologies, dimensions Subject: Political Theory, Politics Series: Oxford Handbooks Collection: Oxford Handbooks Online Conceptualizing Authoritarianism  Ivan Ermako{ Authoritarianism evokes phenomenal realities of di|erent kinds: rules, behaviors, institutional settings, representations, dispositions, and attitudes. Given this lack of xed meaning, the present chapter addresses conceptual issues by considering designs, tools, and argumentative frameworks centered on authoritarian regimes. This practical lens highlights ve modes of conceptualization dened as ways of articulating a type of understanding with an approach and a focus. Cast in ethical terms, the a priori mode relates autocracy to the deprivation of collective choice and capacity. Scale-based approaches measure the degree to which features deemed denitional of democracy are absent or depleted. A typological approach sorts out authoritarian polities by categorizing ways of capturing and managing state power. When we shift to dimensional thinking, the focus is on regime attributes mapping a space of variation. A generic understanding conceptualizes the genus of authoritarian power as unconditional, temporally unbounded and unaccountable. Delineating these ve modes of conceptualization brings into relief contrasts, challenges, and complementarities.
Downloaded from https://academic.oup.com/edited-volume/55828/chapter/504141086 by University of Wisconsin System user on 28 February 2025 Conceptualizing Authoritarianism The problem with substantives that end in “ism” is that they take on multiple appearances and, in so doing, easily cover their tracks. “Authoritarianism” is no exception. The term has been around for quite some time.
It appeared in some book reviews in the 1930s. The sociometric study that Adorno and his coauthors devoted to The Authoritarian Personality published in 1950 granted the term its apparent social-scientic credentials (e.g., Adorno et al. 1950: 52, 228, 232, 234, 246, 269, 560). Edward Shils (1954: 38–41) brought it forward in his critique of this study a few years later. In the mid-1960s, Juan Linz (1964: 293–294) invoked it to challenge the dichotomy between democracy and totalitarianism. Subsequently, it made its way to the cover pages of a few books on political regimes (e.g., O’Donnell 1973; Parming 1975; Malloy 1977; Germani 1978; Perlmutter 1981). Its academic visibility—as reected for instance by article titles—remained roughly constant throughout the two following decades up to the turn of the century, when it started to undergo a dramatic expansion (see the Appendix).
This public recognition notwithstanding, the meaning of the notion remains primarily evocative. The brief genealogy sketched above suggests two broad threads. One denotes a set of dispositions and attitudes revealing a “need for order,” “intolerance,” “aggression,” and “submission” (Altemeyer 1996: 6; Hetherington and Weiler 2009: 34; Stenner and Haidt 2018: 182). The other thread gravitates around the notion of regime. Most often, this second thread is devoid of explicit denitional statements (see the Appendix). Absent denitions, references to the notion proceed through semantic glissandos. Behind the “ism” lurk indistinctly evocations of rules, politics, leaders, conceptions, systems, and proclivities, among others. Implicit references to practices, policies, settings, representations, and psychological proles play a shadow game in the background. Authoritarianism in e|ect announces itself on multiple semantic scenes at once. As a result, it lacks a center of gravity that would hold it together.
To bypass this semantic indeterminacy and make the search for a clear concept more tractable, we could decide to substitute the attribute “authoritarian” for the substantive “authoritarianism.” Our troubles would not end there, however. “Authoritarian” has been assigned to a wide range of referents. To cite only a few: bodies, constitution, equilibrium, governance, institutions, leaders, Leviathan, modernization, organs, personality, regimes, rule, elections, and politics. With so many referents swirling around, it is unclear whether we can pin down a generic meaning that would at once encompass and synthesize the shimmering of connotations generated by this multiplicity of usages.
Given the embarrassment of riches, why not latch onto one referent su}ciently well delimited to lend itself to exercises of conceptual elaboration? “Regime” seems a reasonable choice for this purpose. Systematic empirical investigations have been devoted to “authoritarian regimes.” Furthermore, books that have “authoritarianism” mentioned in their title most often broach the issue of regimes. The empirical category “authoritarian regime” has thus received considerable denitional and empirical attention. As soon as we make this move, though, a profusion of methods, frameworks, and perspectives jostle at the door. Equally troublesome, the term “authoritarian” is frequently used to “refer to all non-democratic regimes” (Huntington 1993: 12; Alvarez et al. 1996: 6). Its meaning then is residual.
The present inquiry takes up the challenges posed by this multiplicity of outlooks, referents, and usages through an examination of conceptual practices as revealed by frameworks, tools, and argumentative logics.
Five modes of conceptualization become discernable when we adopt this practical lens: a priori, scale-based, typological, dimensional, and generic. The a priori mode sets forth a principle of intelligibility of autocracy informed by an ethical perspective centered on the lack of collective capacity and autonomous choices. Scale-based approaches convey a gradual understanding of the authoritarian character of political regimes by gauging the extent to which democratic xtures of various kinds (e.g., rights, freedoms, competitive elections, accountability, deliberation, and pluralism among others) are curtailed or absent. A Downloaded from https://academic.oup.com/edited-volume/55828/chapter/504141086 by University of Wisconsin System user on 28 February 2025 typological understanding accounts for authoritarian regimes’ vast disparity in forms and content by categorizing power holders and their handling of state power. Dimensional analyses characterize authoritarian regimes in light of attributes mapping out a space of variation. A generic conceptualization relates the “authoritarian” character of a regime to a practice of state power presumed to be unconditional, temporally unbounded and unaccountable.
The structure of this chapter follows this short outline. Each section is devoted to the presentation and the discussion of one mode of conceptualization in the aforementioned order. A mode of conceptualization denotes a way of objectifying meaning. It encompasses a type of understanding coupled with an approach and a focus. We identify a mode of conceptualization by asking: which conceptual decision(s) orient and regulate the task of making sense of an object of inquiry? To gauge the ins and outs of each mode indicated above, the following sections adopt the same presentation template: rst, a descriptive account of what the conceptual mode under consideration is up to; second, a review of premises and implications; third, an outline of contributions and challenges.
Why proceed in this fashion? Delineating modes of conceptualization highlights their contrasts with regard to the status of empirics, the use of residual categories, their ontological premises, and the centrality of explicitly stated research problematics. These modes for one thing di|er with regard to the extent to which they are geared to the specication of empirical indicators. Further, some lean on residual categories.
Others do not. In addition, their ontological assumptions can prove far apart. Last, explicit research questions have varying degrees of salience within their ranks. These contrasts in turn underscore contributions and challenges regarding the requirements of empirical assessments, the validity of ontological assumptions, the consistency between usages and premises, the indeterminacy of residual categories, and the demarcation of regimes.
A Priori Let us start with the conceptual coordinates of an a priori understanding. To do so, the denition of autocracy set forth in Hans Kelsen’s General Theory of Law and State is particularly apt. The key word in Kelsen’s conceptual framework is “constitution.” Dening regimes amounts to characterizing constitutions. From Kelsen’s juristic point of view, a constitution stipulates legal norms, and classifying constitutions implies distinguishing the ways in which these constitutions “regulate the creation of a legal order” (Kelsen 1945: 283). The main dichotomy in this respect is between autocracy and democracy. This dichotomy rests on the “idea of political freedom” (Kelsen 1945: 284).
By “political freedom” Kelsen means the capacity to determine oneself through the laws of the polity.
“Politically free is he who is subject to a legal order in the creation of which he participates” (Kelsen 1945: 284). Politically unfree is he who cannot partake in the creation of the legal order to which he is subjected.
The distinction between autocracy and democracy thus hinges on how the citizens of a polity relate to its rules. “Democracy means that the ‘will’ which is represented in the legal order of the State is identical with the wills of the subjects. Its opposite is the bondage of autocracy. There the subjects are excluded from the creation of the legal order” (Kelsen 1945: 284).
Kelsen does not cite Kant’s Metaphysics of Morals. He cites Rousseau’s theorizing the collective will (volonté générale). However, the Kantian subtext and the liation between Rousseau and Kant are obvious enough so that, as Bobbio (1989: 102, 137) does not fail to point out, it makes sense to interpret the contrast between autocracy and democracy as the contrast between heteronomy and autonomy. “An individual is free if what he ‘ought to’ do according to the social order coincides with what he ‘wills to’ do. … Political freedom is liberty, and liberty is autonomy” (Kelsen 1945: 284–285).
Downloaded from https://academic.oup.com/edited-volume/55828/chapter/504141086 by University of Wisconsin System user on 28 February 2025 Insofar as this conception is indebted to a regulative idea abstracted from empirical assessments, it can be labeled “a priori.” In the present instance, the idea regulating the conceptual setup states that the nature of a regime reects how those subjected to its rules relate to these rules. The opposition between autonomy and heteronomy informs the opposition between participation and exclusion. Underlying this approach is an ethical comprehension of politics, provided that we understand “ethical” according to the meaning that Spinoza vests in the concept: we enter the realm of ethics when we reect on how relations a|ect individual and collective capacity (Spinoza 1951 [1670]: ch. XVL; Spinoza 1951: chs. II and III).
This ethical approach informs a political assessment of collective stakes. Herein lies its contribution to the conceptual comprehension of autocracy and, more broadly, political regimes. Recall Kelsen’s understanding of autocracy. Unless actors partake in the elaboration of the rules that regulate their behaviors, they cannot be deemed politically free. Thus, freedom is the regulative principle of democracy. Bondage, on the other hand, constitutes autocracy as a regime of interactions. Ultimately, what is at stake in a polity’s ability to have a say in the content of the legal order is its members’ capacity to assert their freedom. The intelligibility of regimes—their political signicance—is to be found in this ethical assessment.
Kelsen’s General Theory of Law and State is of course not the only possible reference exemplifying the a priori mode. To indicate how wide, from a substantive standpoint, the range of arguments drawing on a priori understandings can be, let us mention Montesquieu’s identication of the ethical principles constitutive of the despotic, the monarchical, and the republican regimes in The Spirit of Laws (Part I, Book III). In despotic regimes, fear prevails, since despots do not feel bound by any law. In monarchies, the existence of rules structuring power mediates ambitions and the search for distinctions. The sense of honor has the upper hand. Republican regimes, on the other hand, rest on the exercise of virtue, since ultimately in these regimes law and order lie in citizens’ hands (Montesquieu 1989: 21–30). Closer to us, Hannah Arendt (1973: ch. 13) portrays terror and the systematic warping of truth through ideology as the principles that constitute totalitarian regimes.
The main challenge for an a priori conception concerns the empirics of its claims. How shall we determine the empirical content of the key terms around which Kelsen’s conceptual constellation revolves: constitution, freedom, heteronomy, and autonomy? We do not know how to apprehend heteronomy en acte.
Nor do we know how to draw the line between autonomy and heteronomy. What shall we make of this distinction when for instance we are considering the case of parliamentary representation? Kelsen (1945: 289) states that a “true relationship of representation” requires that “the representative be legally obliged to execute the will of the represented, and that the fulllment of this obligation be legally guaranteed.” In e|ect, the absence of a “legal obligation to execute the will of the represented” implies the possibility of heteronomy for the represented. Does this mean that political representation without imperative mandates fails the test of “political self-determination”? As we delve into the empirics of the claims, they become more indistinct.
Scale Based Whereas ethical abstractions leave their empirics pending, scale-based approaches anchor themselves right in the thick of empirical appraisals. Here the elaboration of descriptive measures and the scales required for this purpose come rst. No scale, no measurement. A “scale” in the present instance designates a numeral system that makes it possible to assess di|erences in degrees among the items of an empirical class (Duncan 1984: 126; DeVellis 2012: 7). Hence, a scale duly conceived is necessarily graded. “Nominal” and “binary” scales that do not satisfy this requirement are misnomers. That is, categorical distinctions do not constitute a scale properly speaking if the numerals that have been pegged on them do not measure a gradation.
Downloaded from https://academic.oup.com/edited-volume/55828/chapter/504141086 by University of Wisconsin System user on 28 February 2025 However entrenched in the empirics of their cases scale-based approaches might be, they cannot but rely on the guidance of conceptual pointers if only because scale designers have to decide which phenomenal realities are to be measured in the rst place. While regime indices partake in an understanding of authoritarianism as reecting the absence or the depletion of features deemed constitutive of democracy, it is striking to observe how diverse their conceptual make-ups have been. The following overview, which does not claim exhaustivity, is telling.
At the center of the “Index of political democracy” are measures of “political liberties” and “popular sovereignty as represented by elections” (Bollen 1980: 373, 375). The polyarchy scale derived from Dahl’s (1971: 3) notion of polyarchy gauges access to information and the prospect of making meaningful choices through elections in addition to political rights and free and fair elections (Coppedge and Reinicke 1990: 53–54). Vanhanen’s (2000: 253) Index of Democratization combines measures of electoral participation with measures of electoral competition. Freedom House scores assess the extent of political rights and civil freedoms (Gastil 1991: 26, 32–33). The Democracy Barometer aims to operationalize the quality of democracy by distinguishing three “core principles” (freedom, control, and equality) and their respective components (e.g., transparency, participation, and representation in the case of equality) (Bühlmann et al.
2012: 521–529). The Lexical Index of Electoral Democracy di|erentiates regimes in light of the modalities, the institutional signicance and the inclusiveness of elections (Skaaning, Gerring, and Bartusevicius 2015: 1496–1497). Polity5 indicators direct the focus to the competitive and regulated character of political participation, the selection of the executive, and the constraints on the latter’s power (Marshall and Gurr 2020: 42). V-Dem indices owe their raison d’être to ve understandings of democracy adopted as reference frames: the electoral, liberal, participative, deliberative, and egalitarian conceptions (Coppedge et al. 2020: ch. 5). The Economist Intelligence Unit’s Democracy Index includes in its conceptual scope assessments of the functioning of government and political culture in addition to measures of “electoral process and pluralism, political participation, and civil liberties” (Economist Intelligence Unit 2023: 3).1 Given this range of conceptual make-ups, are we still entitled to speak of one mode of conceptualization?
Underpinning the principle of construction of a graded scale is an understanding of families of regimes (democracy, autocracy) as “conceptually continuous” (confer Bollen and Jackman 1989: 613; Bollen 1990: 13; Vanhanen 2000: 257; Pemstein, Meserve, and Melton 2010: 431; Coppedge et al. 2020: 69). Absent the continuity assumption, scores could not pretend to capture di|erences in degrees. The postulate of continuity is thus a shared and distinctive feature of scale-based approaches as much as the epistemic priority granted to measurement. These two common denominators set scale-based approaches apart in modal terms. The continuity postulate furthermore explains why scales constructed to assess the democratic character of regimes—as the labels mentioned above indicate—can be used to appraise their autocratic character as well. Scores are amenable to both democratic and autocratic readings. These depend on which end point of the scale is taken as the reference.
One key implication of the continuity assumption is the lack of clear-cut empirical boundaries. Graded measures convey a di|use representation of “authoritarianism” —in line with the vagueness of the notion —which blurs the boundaries between authoritarian and democratic regimes (Cheibub, Gandhi, and Vreeland 2010: 73). In this representation regimes morph into one another as they move up and down the authoritarian scale. The authoritarian content of a regime by way of consequence has no precise contours (Vanhanen 2000: 257). It designates a reality that varies by degrees in several phenomenal realms at once: rights, freedoms, political participation, executive selection, executive power, the modalities and scope of elections, and various conditions of democratic life.
This di|use representation points to some of the challenges scale-based approaches have been facing. One substantive critique rejects the claim that appraising the authoritarian–democratic distinction is a matter of degrees (Sartori 1991: 248–249; Alvarez et al. 1996: 21). Regimes are either authoritarian or democratic.
They can be neither “half-democratic” (Alvarez et al. 1996: 21) nor “half-authoritarian.” The continuity Downloaded from https://academic.oup.com/edited-volume/55828/chapter/504141086 by University of Wisconsin System user on 28 February 2025 assumption is mistaken. Further, some scales include intermediate categories that, since they are intermediate, are di}cult to interpret (e.g., the category “Intermediate 1” located between the “Parity” and the “Substantial” characterizations of “Constraints on Chief executive” in Marshall et al. 2002: 42).
Additional problems of interpretation arise when contrasted institutional arrangements and political congurations receive the same scores (Gleditsch and Ward 1997: 367; Michalik 2015: 35). Di|erences in score values pose the same problem (Schedler 2006: 11; Way 2016: 2).
On the methodological front, objections pertain to the lack of clarity of operational choices; the possible disconnects between indicators and the phenomenal reality that they are presumed to measure (Bollen 1993: 1210); the reliability of coders’ subjective assessments; and the arbitrary character of aggregation rules, weight coe}cients (in the case of composite indexes), and cuto| points (when practitioners dichotomize continuous indexes) (Munck and Verkuilen 2002: 22–26; Sartori 1991: 249; Treier and Jackman 2008: 202; Vanhanen 2000: 257).
Responses to these various challenges have brought to the fore the pros of this mode of conceptualization.
Consider the charge that a graded conception of regimes lacks validity. For some research topics, such as war initiations, graded measures appear to have more discriminatory power than categorical variables thanks to the ne-grained information about regimes they can provide (Elkins 2000: 296). These contributions give credence to a “pragmatic” stance regarding usage, which advises how to gauge the heuristic relevance of graded measures in light of how much explanatory grasp they yield given the research problem at hand (Huntington 1993: 11; Collier and Adcock 1999: 539; Haggard and Kaufman 2016: 6–7).
Also instructive is how practitioners have engaged methodological challenges head-on through technical renements and further conceptual elaborations. The attempt to operationalize the authoritarian– democratic distinction within the framework of a graded scale requires clarifying the theoretical stakes at play in the determination of thresholds for specic regime facets (e.g., the right of su|rage and the right to compete in elections) (Munck 2006: 32–36). Latent variable analyses make it possible to probe reliability issues across coders and scales (Pemstein, Meserve, and Melton 2010: 427; Coppedge et al. 2020: 79–80).
Reecting on how the components of a scale such as elections, rights, and participation functionally relate to one another as necessary, su}cient, or substitutable conditions provides ways to substantiate the empirical meaning of di|erences in scores (Skaaning, Gerring, and Bartusevicius 2015: 1492–1500), and to analytically justify rules of aggregation (Coppedge et al. 2020: 97–101).2 Typologies Typological distinctions proceed from an ontological understanding that in two respects is at odds with a gradation premise. First, a typology accounts for variation through categorical distinctions. The phenomenal reality of regimes that a typological approach purports to comprehend cannot therefore be conceived in continuous terms. Di|erences are in kind, not of degrees. Furthermore, in a typological framework these di|erences make sense if interpreted in congurational terms. Analysts could hardly set up, let alone theorize, distinct types if they did not assume that categorical distinctions reected bundles of relations characterized by systemic logics.
Typologies of authoritarian regimes abound. Not all of them are analytically motivated or explicit with regard to the rationale presiding over their construction. For instance, Franz Neumann’s (1957: 236–244) distinction between “simple,” “caesaristic,” and “totalitarian” dictatorships is devoid of a research problematic. By contrast, Barbara Geddes (1999: 125–138) conceives the categories “single-party,” “military,” and “personalist” regimes as relevant to investigate authoritarian regimes’ di|erential capacity to last and, conversely, to be terminated through a coup, popular mobilization, or a peaceful transition.
Underlying this line of work is the claim that categorizing the setup of state power, its organization, and its 3 Downloaded from https://academic.oup.com/edited-volume/55828/chapter/504141086 by University of Wisconsin System user on 28 February 2025 structure provides a heuristically fruitful point of entry to analyze its dynamics and more broadly its life cycle (birth, duration, ending) (Geddes 1999: 131–134; Frantz and Ezrow 2011: ch. 2).
A further set of research questions tackles the connections between regime types and outcomes of di|erent kinds, such as development (Przeworski et al. 2000), the propensity to go to war (Lai and Slater 2006; Frantz and Ezrow 2011: ch. 3; Weeks 2014) or the likelihood of policy changes (Frantz and Ezrow 2011: ch. 5).
For instance, in order to probe authoritarian rulers’ propensity for war making, Lai and Slater (2006: 115– 116) construct a fourfold typology that refers to the degree of monopoly of state power (oligarchic versus autocratic) and the basis of the state’s infrastructural power (party or military base) (Slater 2003: 83).
Whether a clearly stated research agenda motivates the adoption of a typological framework or not, typologies of authoritarian regimes in e|ect purport to categorize patterned ways of acceding to, and capturing, state power. To this end, they incorporate two interrelated foci. One identies the “seizure group,” dened as the “small group from which leaders can come and determine who inuences leadership choices and policy” (Roeder 1993: 28; Haber 2006: 696; Geddes, Wright, and Frantz 2014: 314–315; Geddes, Wright, and Frantz 2018: 7–13). Categories proling the most prominent o}ce holders (e.g., “monarchs, military rulers and civilian rulers”) (Gandhi 2008: 19) make this analytical emphasis explicit. The second focus expands the rst one by drawing attention to the management and structure of political power (e.g., the existence of formal regulations, multiple loci of decision-making, and an organizational basis for mobilization) (Alvarez et al. 1996: 16–17).
Observations culled through the prism of a typology, however, are unlikely to be sound if the categories on which this typology rests (1) are not empirically traceable, (2) lack clear-cut boundaries, (3) fail to be mutually exclusive, and (4) do not constitute an exhaustive set, that is, a set such that all cases can be classied (Svolik 2012: 28–29). (1) At stake in the traceability requirement is the identication of reliable and observable indicators. (2) These should also help draw the boundaries between types. (3) Mutual exclusion and unambiguity go hand in hand. If, due to overlapping boundaries, cases can be alternatively assigned to one type or another, the typology cannot fulll its prime classicatory mission. (4) Exhaustivity is the fourth requirement. Uncategorized cases are like lost souls (from a typological perspective).
A second challenge concerns the conceptualization of regimes that appear ambiguous or hybrid because they combine features of both the authoritarian and the democratic types (Diamond 2002: 27; Schedler 2002: 47). The competitive character of elections is a thorny issue in this regard (Schedler 2002: 36; Schedler 2006: 15–16; Schedler 2013: 5–6; Magaloni 2006: 32–34; Brownlee 2007: 12–13). From a procedural standpoint, the fact that contested elections determine who is entitled to exercise executive or legislative functions is enough to characterize a regime as democratic (Alvarez et al. 1996: 5–7; Przeworski et al. 2000: 14–18; Boix 2003: 66; Cheibub, Gandhi, and Vreeland 2010: 69; Svolik 2012: 22). More stringent conceptions, on the other hand, refer to a host of criteria in addition to contested elections such as the rule of law, political rights, civil liberties (Schedler 2002: 37; Ginsburg and Huq 2018: 10–13), the “absence of nonelected ‘tutelary’ authorities (e.g., militaries, monarchies, or religious bodies) that limit elected o}cials’ power to govern, and the existence of a reasonably level playing eld between incumbents and opposition” (Levitsky and Way 2010: 5–6). Accordingly, the failure to fulll one of these criteria is enough to classify a regime in the authoritarian category (Schedler 2013: 80).
The empirical heterogeneity of a residual category, if any, poses a third challenge to a typological mode of conceptualization. It is not rare for typologies of political regimes to include a residual type. This observation applies in the rst place to dichotomies that set authoritarian regimes in contradistinction with one or several denitional features of democracy (Svolik 2012: 17, 90). The meaning of “authoritarian” then becomes relative to, and conditional on, the meaning of “democratic.” As the dening characteristics of democracy become more demanding and the empirical category that they delimit more restrictive, the universe of authoritarian regimes expands. Polychotomous typologies of authoritarian regimes rely on Downloaded from https://academic.oup.com/edited-volume/55828/chapter/504141086 by University of Wisconsin System user on 28 February 2025 residual categories as well. Cheibub, Gandhi, and Vreeland (2010: 89) characterize the “civilian” authoritarian type derived from Gandhi (2008) as the category encompassing all the cases that cannot be adjudicated to the “monarchical” and “military” types. Similarly, Geddes, Wright, and Frantz (2014: 317 present the “personalist” type of authoritarian regimes in residual terms.
A residual category provides a convenient and easy way of dealing with the exhaustivity requirement of a typology since it closes the circle—so to speak—and, as a result, makes the classication unassailable from a logical standpoint (assuming that the other categories are mutually exclusive). SUMMARY:
by WC Wu · Cited by 3 — Modern political regimes can be classified into three main types: democratic, totalitarian and authoritarian regimes. To meet the criterion of

FULL CONTENT:
A Critical Review on the Important Classificatory Concepts of Modern Political Regimes 211 A Critical Review on the Important Classificatory Concepts of Modern Political Regimes Wu, Wen-Cheng * I. The Principles and Criteria of Concept Formation and Classification II. Democracy and Majoritarian Democracy v.s. Consensus Democracy III. Totalitarianism v.s. Authoritarianism IV. Bureaucratic Authoritarianism V. Electoral (Competitive) Authoritarian Regime VI. Conclusion Classification is the beginning of understanding. It is the main method of sorting things out and making sense out of the world we live in. When we classify political phenomena (activities, systems, or institutions) into classes and give each class a general noun we form classificatory concepts. This paper tries to use the basic principles and criteria of * Professor, Department of Political Science, Soochow University, Taipei, Taiwan. E-mail: wjuel@ms33.hinet.net 投稿日期：2015 年5 月11 日；接受刊登日期：2015 年12 月23 日。 東吳政治學報/2015/第三十三卷第四期/頁211-281。 212 東吳政治學報/2015/第三十三卷第四期 classification to critically review the important classificatory concepts of modern political regimes in comparative politics. Modern political regimes can be classified into three main types: democratic, totalitarian and authoritarian regimes. To meet the criterion of exclusiveness, totalitarian regimes and authoritarian regimes, especially one-party authoritarian regimes must be clearly distinguished from each other. Lijphart’s classification of democracy into majoritarian versus consensus democracies sheds light on different political effects of these two modes of democracy, but his classification does not meet the criteria of classification. Authoritarian regimes can be further classified into two types: military authoritarian regimes and one-party authoritarian regimes. O’Donnell’s concept of bureaucratic authoritarianism focuses on the role of bureaucracies and the economic determinants of these regimes. But most of these regimes are established and ruled by the military, it may be more appropriate to just call them military authoritarian regimes. Electoral (or competitive) authoritarian regimes may have quite a few empirical cases, but it is a self contradictory term. It blurs the boundary between democracy and authoritarianism. If some democratic trappings are there just to consolidate authoritarian rule and do not meet the minimal criteria of democracy, it may be better just call them authoritarian regimes. Keywords: classificatory concept, political regime, democratic regime, totalitarian regime, authoritarian regime A Critical Review on the Important Classificatory Concepts of Modern Political Regimes 213 I. The Principles and Criteria of Concept Formation and Classification To call the study of politics ‘political science’, political scientists assume that politics can be studied with scientific principles and methods. The scientific process of theory construction begins with the formation of concepts (Isaak, 1985: 71). Concepts are the building blocks of scientific political study. If a relationship between two concepts can be found, then a generalization can be formulated. A generalization is just a hypothesized relationship between two concepts or two phenomena. Of the two concepts in a generalization, one is called “explanandum,” the concept (or phenomenon) to be explained; another is called “explanans,” the concept (or phenomenon) that explains. Thus, if a generalization is formulated then a political phenomenon is explained and the main concern and primary job of a political scientist is accomplished (Isaak, 1985: 134-136). Simply speaking, a concept is just a general noun that a political scientist gives to a political phenomenon. A phenomenon that interests a political scientist is usually an important and recurrent one. A phenomenon is the common characteristics that manifest and can be observed in some similar political events that occur in different places and at different times. The noun that historians give to a political event is a particular noun. A concept must be a general noun because it 214 東吳政治學報/2015/第三十三卷第四期 usually refers to a recurrent phenomenon, not a particular event. A particular noun needs no definition, but, as general nouns, concepts do need to be defined. The simplest way to define a concept is to write down the important common characteristics of those similar political events because that is what a phenomenon is. Thus, a concept must be defined empirically, i.e., with observable characteristics. Concepts in political science are general nouns. They all have the level of generality. At the first stage of theory construction-concept formation, political scientists encounter the first dilemma of political science that is to determine the level of generality of a concept and the range or scope of the phenomenon they want to describe and explain. The formation of a concept has two steps: first, give the phenomenon a noun, and then second, define it. Both steps have to do with determining the level of generality. A more or a less general noun can be chosen and given to a phenomenon, e.g. political development is more general than democratic transition. The democratic transition in Taiwan is easier to describe and explain. Political development in Taiwan is too general. That includes all political phenomena that have ever occurred in Taiwan. We can not explain all political phenomena in Taiwan, it would be impossible. We can only explain one phenomenon at a time. After forming a concept, we encounter the dilemma again when we define it. There are two kinds of definitions: broad definition and narrow definition. The broader the definition the more general the concept becomes and the narrower the definition and the less general A Critical Review on the Important Classificatory Concepts of Modern Political Regimes 215 the concept becomes. For example, if we define ‘revolution’ as major and drastic changes in politics, that is a very broad definition. With such a definition, lifting of martial law in 1987, the first direct election of the president in 1996, and the first rotation of power in 2000 can all be described as revolutionary events. If we define revolution as mass participation in an uprising, involving large-scale political violence, an old or traditional regime is toppled and a new or modern regime with new political systems is established, that is a narrower definition. With such a definition, the above mentioned events can be excluded from the phenomenon we call the revolution. The higher level of generality of the concept and the broader of its definition the wider the range and scope the concept and phenomenon covers, yet, it will be more difficult to refer to tangible and observable things in the empirical world. However, we can not lower the generality of the concept and define it so narrowly as to become a particular noun, then it is no longer a concept. After World War II the geographical scope of political science has been greatly expanded to include developing as well as developed areas. Political scientists find many concepts formed before the War to describe phenomena in the Western World are not applicable to the developing areas in the Third World. There are mainly two solutions: one is broadening the meaning (definition) of existing concepts to cover developing areas and the other is forming new concepts with the high generality that are applicable to the whole world. The wider the world we want to cover, the more we need concepts that are able to 216 東吳政治學報/2015/第三十三卷第四期 travel and the more we have to resort to conceptual stretching, i.e., to general yet vague conceptualization. The result of concept stretching is that our gains in extensional coverage tend to be matched by losses in connotative precision. It appears that we can cover more only by saying less, and by saying less in a far less precise manner. As we climb or descend along the ladder (level) of abstraction (generality) we obtain different degrees of inclusiveness (scope) and, conversely, specificity. Perhaps the medium level of abstraction is the best choice because generalizations are usually formulated with concepts at this level (Sartori, 1970: 1034-1041). All sound and useful concepts in political science must have two imports: empirical import and systematic import (Hempel, 1952: 39-45). Empirical import means that concepts must be linked with the world of observation, the empirical world. The concept must be defined with observable, tangible, and/or measurable things in the empirical world. This kind of definition is called an empirical definition. As mentioned above, the more general the concept is the more difficult it is be empirically defined. Systematic import has to do with the relationships between concepts. To be useful, a concept cannot be formed in isolation. It is of little use if a concept has no relationship with any other concept. When a concept is hypothesized to have a certain relationship with another concept, the concept has systematic import and we formulate a generalization. With a generalization, we have an explanation and without a generalization there is no explanation. That is why we say a concept is of little use if A Critical Review on the Important Classificatory Concepts of Modern Political Regimes 217 it has no systematic import (Issak, 1985: 89-90). A generalization is just a hypothesis, it needs to be verified. In fact, most academic researches done by political scientists are to verify existing generalizations and theories. To be verifiable both two concepts in the generalization must have an empirical import that is they must be empirically defined. Only with an empirical import can we be sure that the two cases we choose to verify the generalization is one occurrence of the phenomenon that explains and one occurrence of the phenomenon that is explained. Concepts are formed to designate (give general nouns to) and describe (identify and define with observable characteristics of) political phenomena. Concepts in political science have many functions. In addition to designation and description, concepts can be used to classify, compare and measure. There are several kinds of concepts with different functions and usefulness. More specifically, there are classificatory, comparative, and quantitative concepts, and the most important concept among these three is the classificatory concept. Many, perhaps most, concepts in political science are classificatory concepts. Classification is the beginning of understanding. We do not know the nature of many things and phenomena occurring around us. But if we can classify them into different classes we begin to understand a little about them. Classification is the main method for sorting out things and making sense out of this world we live in. When we classify political phenomena into classes and give each class a general noun we form 218 東吳政治學報/2015/第三十三卷第四期 classificatory concepts (Hempel, 1952). Classificatory concepts are also concepts. To be useful a classificatory concept must also have empirical import and systematic import. In addition to empirical import and systematic import, a good classification must also meet two more specific criteria: exhaustiveness and exclusiveness (Isaak, 1985: 84; Sartori, 1970: 1038). Exhaustiveness (or inclusiveness) means when we classify things every every case can be put into one class. There are no leftovers, nothing (or no case) is excluded from the classification. Exclusiveness means when we classify things one thing or one case can only be put into one class, nothing (or no case) can be put into two or more classes. Exclusiveness also means that each class must be very different from other classes. Actually, the basic principle of classification is that we should put similar things in one class and different things in different classes. Simply speaking, the two criteria of classification mean that every case shall fall into one class and one class only. A good classification helps us to understand. However, a bad classification or classificatory concept only confuses. Classificatory concepts are not (or different from) comparative concepts and quantitative concepts. Classificatory concepts are either-or types of concepts, not more-or-less types of concepts. Different classes are different in kind, not different in degree. “What is” qualitative questions cannot be changed into “how much” quantitative questions. An object (or a case) can not be put into two classes, not even a mixed or hybrid of two classes. It violates the A Critical Review on the Important Classificatory Concepts of Modern Political Regimes 219 principle of exclusiveness. Classificatory concepts are required to be mutually exclusive. Of course, we can attach numerical values (or an index) to a concept. But the prior question is “how much” in “what”. Concept formation must come before quantification (Sartori, 1970: 1036-1037). There are two kinds of classification: dichotomous classification and multiple classifications. Political scientists can form a concept of democracy and then classify all political regimes as either democratic or undemocratic. We can define the concept, democracy, with observable characteristics; such as popular elections of public officials and fair competition between political parties. Then we can place all regimes that have these characteristics in the category of the democratic regime, and all those regimes that do not have these characteristics in the category of the undemocratic regime. The dichotomous classification has only two classes while multiple classification has more than two classes. Aristotle classified ancient city states in Greece into three main types: monarchy (rule by one man), oligarchy (rule by a few men) and democracy (rule by many people), and then each of the three types were further divided into two types: good (rule for public interest) and bad (rule for personal interest), altogether six types. This is a good example of multiple classification. Sometimes a political scientist finds a classification is not inclusive, some items are left out or unclassified. If those items have similar characteristics, a new classificatory concept can be formed; if they do not have enough similar characteristics, a 220 東吳政治學報/2015/第三十三卷第四期 miscellaneous category can be created. Either way, the classification is a weak one and is not very useful. The above-mentioned points are the important principles and criteria of classification and formation of classificatory concepts. This paper tries to review the important classificatory concepts of modern political regimes in comparative politics, using the above mentioned basic principles and criteria of concept formation and classification. Political science is full of many classificatory concepts of political regime. This paper will review the three major types of modern political regimes, namely democracy, totalitarianism, and authoritarianism. This paper will also critically review the two subtypes of democratic regime-majoritarian and consensus democracies and two subtypes of authoritarian regimes-bureaucratic authoritarian and electoral (or competitive) authoritarian regimes because these subtypes have gained wide currency and usage among many political scientists. Although this article is only a theoretical review article, not an empirical research paper, clarifying these classificatory concepts can pave/create a sounder theoretical foundation for future empirical research. II. Democracy and Majoritarian Democracy v.s. Consensus Democracy By and large political science is still at the beginning stage of understanding. The nature of politics is difficult to understand and the A Critical Review on the Important Classificatory Concepts of Modern Political Regimes 221 reason why political phenomena occur is very difficult to explain so all scholars can do is to classify phenomena into classes (types or categories), the formation of classificatory concepts. If we can classify a phenomenon into different types, it seems that we begin to understand the phenomenon a little bit. Some phenomena are easy to classify, e.g. the form of state can be classified into two types: monarchy and republic. A monarchy is a kingdom or an empire. The head of state, the king or the emperor, is hereditary and usually sits on the throne for life. In a republic, the head of state, usually called the president, is elected and serves with limited terms and tenure. This is a straight forward classification with little confusion. A monarchy is not necessarily an undemocratic regime. Monarchy system can be further divided into two types: absolute monarchy and constitutional monarchy. An absolute monarchy, where an emperor or a king rules with absolute or real political power, is a traditional and undemocratic regime. A constitutional monarchy, where the emperor or king becomes the figure head of state with only ceremonial duties but no real political power, can still establish a constitutional democracy. Likewise, a republic is not necessarily a democratic regime. A totalitarian or an authoritarian regime can also be established in a republic. On the other hand, political regime: the method of government, how the nation is governed, or how the government exercises its power and the relationship of people to their government, is more difficult to classify. Aristotle’s classification is for the city states in Greece in 222 東吳政治學報/2015/第三十三卷第四期 ancient times and is not applicable to modern political regimes. Political scientists, especially in the field of comparative politics, nowadays classify modern political regimes into three main types: totalitarian, authoritarian, and democratic regimes. These three classificatory concepts were formed by different scholars. The classification is not done by one scholar, but a consensus among most political scientists. Juan Linz is perhaps the first scholar who put the three types together as the three main types of modern political regimes (Linz, 1975). Since it is not classified by one scholar with the same classificatory criteria, the best thing we can do is to discuss these three classificatory concepts one by one. Although democracy is a modern type of political regime, it was first established by the United States of America in 1787, the concept was first formed by Aristotle a long time ago. Athens, a city-state in ancient Greece, briefly practiced a democratic form of government by holding public meetings attended by all citizens in the city to decide public affairs, an ancient form of direct democracy. In the past, many scholars have given many different definitions to democracy. “Classical democratic theories” put forth by various scholars and thinkers before the end of the 19th century are mainly normative theories. Mass participation by all citizens in decision-making or policy-making on all public affairs is feasible only in a small town meeting, village or neighborhood community meeting, but is unrealistic and infeasible in a large country. In a large country, especially a complex industrialized society, a small group of public A Critical Review on the Important Classificatory Concepts of Modern Political Regimes 223 officials must be elected to participate in policy-making. Democracy is necessarily a representative democracy. Most scholars of “classical democratic theories” gave democracy normative definitions, according to what ideal situation they thought democracy or politics should be, Joseph Schumpeter summarized the eighteenth century philosophy of democracy into a final definition: “the democratic method is that institutional arrangement for arriving at political decisions which realizes the common good by making the people itself decide issues through the election of individuals who are to assemble in order to carry out its will.” (Schumpeter, 1943: 250). Although this definition corresponds with representative democracy and is partly empirical because in the 18th century parliaments and congress had been established in America and several West European countries, but it still is a normative and unrealistic definition. Schumpeter was right to point out that there is no such thing as the common good and general will of the people that all people could agree on and the fact that to different individuals and groups the common good and the general will are bound to mean different things (Schumpeter, 1943: 250-268). Classical theories of democracy were normative and value-laden, whereas modern political theories are empirical and value-free, grounded firmly in the facts of political life. Schumpeter, arguably the first scholar of “modern theories of democracy” considered that the facts showed that classical democratic theories were in need of revision so [that] he put forward a new definition of democracy. He 224 東吳政治學報/2015/第三十三卷第四期 argued that democracy is not an ideal situation and offered the following as an empirical and realistic definition of democracy: “That institutional arrangement for arriving at political decisions in which individuals acquire the power to decide by means of a competitive struggle for the people’s vote.” (Schumpeter, 1943: 269). Schumpeter considered the central participatory and decision- making role of the people in the “classical democratic theories” rested on an empirically unrealistic foundation. In Schumpeter’s theory, participation by the people has no special or central role in the democratic method. It is the competition for leadership-the power to decide or the right to rule-that is the distinctive feature of democracy. He did not even regard universal suffrage necessary (Schumpeter, 1943: 244-245). All that is required is that enough citizens turn out to vote to keep the electoral machinery-the institutional arrangement-working. The focus of his theory is on the minority of leaders. It is the competition between leaders for votes that is the major characteristics in his democratic method (Schumpeter, 1943: 272-283). Robert Dahl, another famous scholar of “modern democratic theories”, pointed out that democracy is made up of at least two dimensions: in addition to competition (he called it “public contestation”); there is another dimension: participation (he called it “the right to participate”). In Dahl’s view, democracy is a political method, a list of institutional arrangements that center on the electoral process. Elections are central to the democratic method because they provide the mechanism through which the people can “control” the A Critical Review on the Important Classificatory Concepts of Modern Political Regimes 225 leaders. Leaders are not a single group of leaders but several (plural) groups of leaders. Thus, he called his theory of democracy polyarchy: the rule of multiple minorities. On the other side of the electoral process, the competition between leaders for the votes of the people, the fact the people can switch their support from one group of leaders to another ensures that leaders are “relatively responsive” to the people. Dahl listed several requirements for a democracy and on top was the freedom to form and join organizations, namely political parties. Public contestation is not just a competition between individual candidates, but between different groups of leaders, between political parties. Thus, elections (of public officials) and electoral participation (by the electorate) and competition (between parties and candidates) become the two most distinctive features in a democracy (Dahl, 1971: 1-9). Both Schumpeter and Dahl did not put forward a democratic theory that requires maximum participation from ordinary people because they knew most people tend to be disinterested and apathetic about politics. Dahl also recognized that a relatively small proportion of individuals in any form of social organizations will take up decision-making responsibilities. Most people can only participate in politics through voting during elections, and even in an election some people just do not go to vote (Dahl, 1971). Sartori called the democratic theory put forth by Schumpeter and Dahl the “competitive theory of democracy.” Actually, we can also call this theory of democracy the “electoral theory of democracy” because competition 226 東吳政治學報/2015/第三十三卷第四期 and election are just two sides of the same democratic coin. Sartori also called democracy defined by Schumpeter and Dahl “minimal democracy” not the maximal democracy (Sartori, 1987: 152-156). What Schumpeter and Dahl offered in their definitions are the basic or lowest criteria of democracy? These are basic criteria, which means once a regime meets these criteria, it is a democratic regime. It also means that when a regime fails to meet these criteria it is not a democratic regime. The definitions they gave are clear-cut criteria; they distinguish or separate a democratic from an undemocratic regime clearly. They meet the “exclusiveness” criterion of classification. Only by meeting the simple yet strict criterion of exclusiveness in concept formation and classification can the concepts formed by political scientists be useful in analyzing and explaining political phenomena. For example, the political phenomenon-democratic transition is a transition from an undemocratic regime (either a totalitarian regime or an authoritarian regime) into a democratic regime. Democratic transition is a stage, a period or an interval of time; it has a beginning point and an ending point. Before the beginning point the regime must be an undemocratic regime and after the ending point (or completion point) the regime must be a democratic regime. If we do not have a clear-cut definition of democracy, we can not be sure whether a democratic transition has actually occurred and we can not know when a democratic transition began and when it completed. If we use a normative definition of democracy which usually entails high criteria for democracy, then the democratic transition can never be A Critical Review on the Important Classificatory Concepts of Modern Political Regimes 227 completed and the phenomenon (or the case) can not be pinned down for analysis and explanation. Arend Lijphart, a renowned political scientist, divides democratic regimes into two subtypes: majoritarian democracy and consensus democracy. He uses different institutions and rules to distinguish the majoritarian (also called Westminster) model from the consensus model of democracy. The Westminster model (in the United Kingdom) is characterized by (1) concentration of executive power in one-party and bare-majority cabinet; (2) cabinet dominance; (3) two-party system; (4) majoritarian and disproportional system of elections; (5) interest group pluralism; (6) unitary and centralized government; (7) concentration of legislative power in a unicameral legislature; (8) constitutional flexibility; (9) absence of judicial review; (10) a central bank controlled by the executive (Lijphart, 1999: 10-21); and the consensus model (in Switzerland and Belgium) is characterized by (1) executive power-sharing in a broad coalition cabinet; (2) executive legislative balance of power; (3) multi-party system; (4) proportional representation; (5) interest group corporatism; (6) federal and decentralized government; (7) strong bicameralism; (8) constitutional rigidity; (9) judicial review; (10) central bank independence (Lijphart, 1999: 34-41). Lijphart argues that the majoritarian model accepts majority rule only as the minimum requirement, the model concentrates political power in the hands of a bare majority and often merely a plurality instead of majority; whereas the consensus model seeks to maximize 228 東吳政治學報/2015/第三十三卷第四期 the size of these majorities. The majoritarian model of democracy is exclusive, competitive, and adversarial, whereas the consensus model is characterized by inclusiveness, bargaining, and compromise. Apparently, he prefers consensus democracy much more than majoritarian democracy because the former has only a bare or the minimal majority, implying minimal democracy, whereas the later has maximal majority, implying maximal democracy (Lijphart, 1999: 2). Before the 1960s, the standard-bearer of democratic systems was the Anglo-Saxon majoritarian or Westminster model. The majoritarian model was regarded as the best and most democratic, all other forms of democratic governance were regarded as inferior and less democratic. This assumption was challenged and refuted by Lijphart. Thus, Lijphart’s works have incited heated debates and have been subject to repeated criticisms. Anré Kaiser is right to criticize that Lijphart did not discuss the constitutional engineering process that lead to different political institutions, but he is wrong to criticize that Lijphart did not discuss the effects of different types of democracy on policy outputs (Kaiser, 1997). Actually, Kaiser is right on both points, Lijphart did not discuss the effects of different types of democracy in his 1984 edition entitled “Democracies: Patterns of Majoritarian and Consensus Government in Twenty-One Countries” (Lijphart, 1984), but he added two chapters (15 and 16) to discuss it in his 1999 edition entitled “Patterns of Democracy: Government Forms and Performance in Thirty-Six Countries” (Lijphart, 1999). Lijphart does argue that the two different A Critical Review on the Important Classificatory Concepts of Modern Political Regimes 229 patterns of democracy have different policy performance. He finds out that consensus democracies have the better record on effective government and policy-making. He concludes that consensus democracies do clearly outperform majoritarian democracies with regard to the quality of democracy and democratic representation as well as to what he has called the kindness and gentleness of their public policy orientations. He also argues that the fe

enriched_points.expanded_content: Regime classification hinges on clear criteria, distinguishing authoritarian regimes by the absence of democratic features, such as free elections and civil liberties (Glasius, 2018).
Indicators for political stability encompass regime survival rates, institutional robustness, and the ruling party's ability to maintain control over security forces and policy (Wu, 2015).

Efforts to measure democratization involve assessing electoral processes, civil rights, and political participation, often using indices that gauge the degree of democratic features present (Ermakoff, 2024).
